mnemonics: Composition Order: 'SOUP' - Substitute Outside function's variable with the Output of the Underlying (inside) function, Provided it's valid.
Inverse Proof: 'Swap & Solve' - Swap x and y, then Solve for y to find the inverse.
One-to-One Check: 'H-Line Test' - A function is one-to-one if no Horizontal line hits the graph more than once.
keyPoints: Function composition (f ∘ g)(x) is defined as f(g(x)). The output of g must be in the domain of f.
A function must be bijective (both one-to-one and onto) to have an inverse.
The inverse function f⁻¹ undoes the action of f, so f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(y)) = y.
commonMistakes: Assuming the composition (f ∘ g) is the same as (g ∘ f). Composition is not commutative.
Forgetting to verify the domain and codomain restrictions when finding an inverse function.
Assuming every function has an inverse; only bijective functions do.
